findings.title: Performance Regression Risk
findings.description: The recent performance regression in dashboard load times and API response times poses a critical risk to user satisfaction and retention. Immediate action is required to address the underlying causes.
assumptions: The performance regressions are directly linked to the recent deployments and migrations.

evidence.excerpt: Dashboard load time regressed from 1.2s to 4.8s after v3.2 deploy
API response times degraded 3x after database migration

recommendations: Prioritize fixing the performance regressions in the dashboard and API.

findings.title: Dependency on Database Optimization
findings.description: The performance of the API is heavily dependent on optimizing the database queries post-migration. Addressing the query planner regression is essential for restoring API performance.
assumptions: The database migration has introduced inefficiencies that need to be resolved.

evidence.excerpt: The migration from PostgreSQL 14 to 16 introduced a query planner regression

recommendations: Collaborate with the database team to analyze and optimize the query planner.

findings.title: Implement Performance Regression Detection
findings.description: To prevent future regressions, implement a performance regression detection mechanism in the CI pipeline that benchmarks performance metrics against a baseline.
assumptions: Automated performance testing can effectively catch regressions before deployment.

evidence.excerpt: Implement a benchmark suite that runs on every PR

recommendations: Develop and integrate the performance benchmarking suite into the CI pipeline.

findings.title: Build Customer-Facing Status Page
findings.description: Creating a public status page will enhance customer transparency regarding performance metrics and incidents, improving user trust and satisfaction.
assumptions: Customers will benefit from real-time visibility into performance metrics.

evidence.excerpt: Build a public status page that shows real-time p50/p95/p99 latencies

recommendations: Design and implement the customer-facing status page.

findings.title: Memory Leak in WebSocket Connections
findings.description: The memory leak in real-time WebSocket connections poses a risk of service outages, affecting customer experience. This needs immediate attention to prevent OOM crashes.
assumptions: The memory leak is the primary cause of the OOM crashes.

evidence.excerpt: Real-time monitoring dashboard connections leak approximately 2MB per hour per connection.

recommendations: Investigate and resolve the memory leak in the WebSocket server.

summary: The analysis reveals critical performance regressions affecting both the dashboard and API, necessitating immediate remediation efforts. Key dependencies include database optimizations and addressing memory leaks in WebSocket connections. Recommendations include implementing performance regression detection in the CI pipeline and building a customer-facing status page for transparency.
